title: Tapping for I Don't Know What Brings Me Joy
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the blankness when someone asks what you love
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for the woman who has lost touch with what she actually enjoys: the busyness that replaced it, the forgetting, the blank when someone asks what brings her joy. Taps through the loss and toward permission to find out again.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when you cannot easily name what brings you joy or what you want for yourself — particularly when busyness or long-term caregiving has crowded it out.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for the woman who draws a blank when asked what brings her joy. This is more common than it sounds. The things that once brought joy were slowly displaced by the demands of a full life, and at some point the knowing went quiet. This sequence works on the grief of the displacement and the permission to find out again.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though I don't know what brings me joy anymore, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though the question draws a blank and that feels embarrassing to admit, I accept this is where I am right now.
Even though I lost touch with what I love and I'm not sure how to find it again, I'm willing to give myself permission to look.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: I don't know what I want for myself.
Side of Eye: The things I used to love that I stopped doing.
Under Eye: The busyness that replaced all of it.
Under Nose: The pressure to have hobbies, to have passions, to know.
Chin: The blankness when someone asks what I love.
Collarbone: The performance of enjoying things when I'm not sure I feel it.
Under Arm: The grief of not knowing what my own joy feels like anymore.
Top of Head: The forgetting I didn't notice happening.
Reframe
Eyebrow: Not knowing is a starting point, not a verdict.
Side of Eye: The things I used to love are still available. I can try one.
Under Eye: Joy doesn't have to be large or consistent. Small things count.
Under Nose: There is no wrong answer. I get to find out again.
Chin: I don't need certainty to start. I just need one small experiment.
Collarbone: My joy is mine to find. No one else can define it for me.
Under Arm: The forgetting can be reversed. That's what finding out is.
Top of Head: I give myself permission to discover this again, without a deadline.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
